---
title: "HW 6: Modelos 5"
subtitle: "Tarea 6"
author:
  - name: "Rigoberto J. Martinez Madriz"
    affiliations:
      - name: "ITESO"
      - name: "Quantitative Finance"
  - name: "Keysi Fuentes Piña"
    affiliations:
      - name: "ITESO"
      - name: "Quantitative Finance"
date: "2025-03-01"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: right
    toc-title: "Contenido"
    self-contained: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contenido"
number-sections: true
---

## Convenciones (para que las cuentas sean consistentes)

Trabajaremos con incrementos y su paso a diferenciales:

$$
\Delta S_t := S_{t+\Delta t}-S_t,
\qquad
\Delta f := f(S_{t+\Delta t})-f(S_t),
\qquad
\Delta t \to 0 \Rightarrow (\Delta t,\Delta S_t,\Delta f)\to (dt,dS_t,df).
$$

Además, usaremos las reglas estándar del cálculo de Itô (en el sentido de orden de magnitud):

$$
(dt)^2=0,\qquad dt\,dW_t=0,\qquad (dW_t)^2=dt.
$$

En particular, todo término de orden estrictamente mayor que $dt$ se descarta (por ejemplo $dt^2$, $dt\,dW_t$, $dW_t^3$, etc.).

---

## 1. (Univariada, $k=3$) Taylor para $f(S_t)$ y aplicación a $f(S_t)=\ln(S_t)$

### 1.1 Taylor de orden 3

Sea $f(S_t)$ una función suficientemente diferenciable y tomemos un punto base $S_1$ y un punto cercano $S_t$.

La expansión de Taylor alrededor de $S_1$ hasta orden $k=3$ es:

$$
f(S_t)
=
f(S_1)
+ \frac{\partial f(S_1)}{\partial S_t}(S_t-S_1)
+ \frac{1}{2!}\frac{\partial^{2} f(S_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}(S_t-S_1)^{2}
+ \frac{1}{3!}\frac{\partial^{3} f(S_1)}{\partial S_t^{3}}(S_t-S_1)^{3}.
$$

Si $S_t \to S_1$, entonces definimos:

$$
S_t-S_1=\Delta S_t,
\qquad
f(S_t)-f(S_1)=\Delta f(S_t).
$$

Por tanto:

$$
\Delta f(S_t)
=
\frac{\partial f(S_1)}{\partial S_t}\Delta S_t
+ \frac{1}{2!}\frac{\partial^{2} f(S_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}(\Delta S_t)^{2}
+ \frac{1}{3!}\frac{\partial^{3} f(S_1)}{\partial S_t^{3}}(\Delta S_t)^{3}.
$$

Cuando $\Delta S_t \to 0$:

$$
\Delta S_t=dS_t,
\qquad
\Delta f(S_t)=df(S_t).
$$

Sustituyendo:

$$
df(S_t)
=
\frac{\partial f(S_t)}{\partial S_t}dS_t
+ \frac{1}{2!}\frac{\partial^{2} f(S_t)}{\partial S_t^{2}}(dS_t)^{2}
+ \frac{1}{3!}\frac{\partial^{3} f(S_t)}{\partial S_t^{3}}(dS_t)^{3}.
$$

### 1.2 Caso particular: $f(S_t)=\ln(S_t)$

Derivadas (exactamente como en tus notas, solo estandarizando el símbolo $S$):

$$
\frac{d}{dS_t}\ln(S_t)=\frac{1}{S_t},
\qquad
\frac{d^{2}}{dS_t^{2}}\ln(S_t)=-\frac{1}{S_t^{2}},
\qquad
\frac{d^{3}}{dS_t^{3}}\ln(S_t)=\frac{2}{S_t^{3}}.
$$

Entonces:

$$
d\ln(S_t)
=
\frac{1}{S_t}dS_t
+ \frac{1}{2!}\left(-\frac{1}{S_t^{2}}\right)(dS_t)^{2}
+ \frac{1}{3!}\left(\frac{2}{S_t^{3}}\right)(dS_t)^{3}.
$$


### 1.3 Sustitución del proceso de difusión (GBM) y expansión completa

Tomamos el proceso de difusión:

$$
dS_t=(\mu_R)S_tdt+S_t\sigma dW_t.
$$

Sustituyendo en la expresión anterior:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d\ln(S_t)
&=
\frac{1}{S_t}\Big[(\mu_R)S_tdt + S_t\sigma dW_t\Big]
-\frac{1}{2S_t^{2}}\Big[(\mu_R)S_tdt + S_t\sigma dW_t\Big]^{2}
+\frac{1}{6}\Big[(\mu_R)S_tdt + S_t\sigma dW_t\Big]^{3}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Primeros términos:

$$
\frac{1}{S_t}\Big[(\mu_R)S_tdt + S_t\sigma dW_t\Big]
=
\mu_Rdt+\sigma dW_t.
$$

Ahora expandimos el cuadrado:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Big[(\mu_R)S_tdt + S_t\sigma dW_t\Big]^2
&=
(\mu_RS_tdt)^{2}+2(\mu_RS_tdt)(S_t\sigma dW_t)+(S_t\sigma dW_t)^{2} \\
&=
\mu_R^{2}S_t^{2}dt^{2}
+2\mu_R\sigma S_t^{2}dt\,dW_t
+S_t^{2}\sigma^{2}dW_t^{2}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Aplicando Itô:

$$
dt^{2}=0,\qquad dt\,dW_t=0,\qquad dW_t^{2}=dt
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\Big[(\mu_R)S_tdt + S_t\sigma dW_t\Big]^2 = S_t^{2}\sigma^{2}dt.
$$

Ahora expandimos el cubo:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Big[(\mu_R)S_tdt + S_t\sigma dW_t\Big]^3
&=
(\mu_RS_tdt)^{3}
+3(\mu_RS_tdt)^{2}(S_t\sigma dW_t)
+3(\mu_RS_tdt)(S_t\sigma dW_t)^{2}
+(S_t\sigma dW_t)^{3}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Reescribiendo cada término por orden:

- $(\mu_RS_tdt)^3$ es proporcional a $dt^3$ y se descarta.
- $3(\mu_RS_tdt)^2(S_t\sigma dW_t)$ es proporcional a $dt^2 dW_t$ y se descarta.
- $3(\mu_RS_tdt)(S_t\sigma dW_t)^2$ es proporcional a $dt(dW_t^2)=dt(dt)=dt^2$ y se descarta.
- $(S_t\sigma dW_t)^3$ es proporcional a $dW_t^3=dW_t(dW_t^2)=dW_tdt$ y se descarta.

Por tanto:

$$
\Big[(\mu_R)S_tdt + S_t\sigma dW_t\Big]^3 = 0
\quad\text{(en el cálculo de Itô, al truncar a orden }dt\text{)}.
$$

Sustituyendo todo:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d\ln(S_t)
&=
\Big[\mu_Rdt+\sigma dW_t\Big]
-\frac{1}{2S_t^{2}}\Big[S_t^{2}\sigma^{2}dt\Big]
+\frac{1}{6}\Big[0\Big].
\end{aligned}
$$

Y finalmente:

$$
d\ln(S_t)
=
\Big[\mu_R-\frac{1}{2}\sigma^{2}\Big]dt+\sigma dW_t.
$$


### 1.4 Por qué “desaparecen” los términos con $k>2$ 

Sabemos que podemos expresar el diferencial de una función como:

$$
df(S_t)
=
\frac{\partial f(S_t)}{\partial S}dS_t
+\frac{1}{2!}\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_t)}{\partial S^{2}}(dS_t)^{2}
+\frac{1}{3!}\frac{\partial^{3}f(S_t)}{\partial S^{3}}(dS_t)^{3}
+\frac{1}{4!}\frac{\partial^{4}f(S_t)}{\partial S^{4}}(dS_t)^{4}
+\ldots
$$

y que “si $(dS_t)^3=0$ entonces todo lo demás también”.

La forma rigurosa de decirlo en Itô es: **solo conservamos términos hasta orden $dt$**.  
Como $dS_t=\mu S_tdt+\sigma S_t dW_t$, se tiene que:

$$
(dS_t)^2 = (\sigma S_t)^2(dW_t)^2 = \sigma^2 S_t^2 dt,
$$

y cualquier potencia $(dS_t)^k$ con $k\ge 3$ es de orden mayor que $dt$ (por ejemplo $dt^{3/2}$ o $dt^2$), por lo que se descarta en la expansión de Itô:

$$
(dS_t)^k = 0
\quad\text{(en el sentido de truncación de Itô a orden }dt\text{), para }k>2.
$$


## 2.Taylor para $f(S_t,t)$ y caso $dS_t=S_t\sigma dW_t$

### 2.1 Taylor de orden 2

Sea $f(S,t)$ una función continua y diferenciable, con punto base $(S_1,t_1)$ y punto cercano $(S_t,t)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(S_t,t)
&=
f(S_1,t_1)
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t}(S_t-S_1)
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t}(t-t_1) \\
&\quad
+\frac{1}{2!}\Big[
\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}(S_t-S_1)^{2}
+\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t^{2}}(t-t_1)^{2}
+2\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t\partial t}(S_t-S_1)(t-t_1)
\Big].
\end{aligned}
$$

Cuando $t_1\to t$ y $S_1\to S_t$, definimos:

$$
t-t_1=\Delta t,\qquad S_t-S_1=\Delta S_t,\qquad f(S_t,t)-f(S_1,t_1)=\Delta f(S_t,t).
$$

Por tanto:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f(S_t,t)
&=
\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t}\Delta S_t
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t_t}\Delta t \\
&\quad
+\frac{1}{2!}\Big[
\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}(\Delta S_t)^{2}
+\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t^{2}}(\Delta t)^{2}
+2\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t\partial t}(\Delta S_t)(\Delta t)
\Big].
\end{aligned}
$$

Al pasar al límite $\Delta t\to 0$:

$$
\Delta t=dt,\qquad \Delta S_t=dS_t,\qquad \Delta f(S_t,t)=df(S_t,t),
$$

obtenemos:

$$
\begin{aligned}
df(S_t,t)
&=
\frac{\partial f(S_t,t)}{\partial S_t}dS_t
+\frac{\partial f(S_t,t)}{\partial t}dt \\
&\quad
+\frac{1}{2}\Big[
\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_t,t)}{\partial S_t^{2}}(dS_t)^{2}
+\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_t,t)}{\partial t^{2}}(dt)^{2}
+2\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_t,t)}{\partial S_t\partial t}(dS_t)(dt)
\Big].
\end{aligned}
$$

Por Itô, $(dt)^2=0$ y $(dS_t)(dt)=0$, entonces queda:

$$
df(S_t,t)
=
\frac{\partial f(S_t,t)}{\partial S}dS_t
+\frac{\partial f(S_t,t)}{\partial t}dt
+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_t,t)}{\partial S^{2}}(dS_t)^{2}.
$$

### 2.2 Sustituyendo $dS_t=S_t\sigma dW_t$

Si el proceso de difusión es:

$$
dS_t=S_t\sigma dW_t,
$$

entonces:

$$
(dS_t)^2=(S_t\sigma dW_t)^2=S_t^{2}\sigma^{2}(dW_t)^2=S_t^{2}\sigma^{2}dt.
$$

Sustituyendo:

$$
df(S_t,t)
=
\frac{\partial f(S_t,t)}{\partial S_T}dS_t
+\frac{\partial f(S_t,t)}{\partial t}dt
+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_t,t)}{\partial S_t^{2}}(S_t^{2}\sigma^{2}dt).
$$

Equivalente (forma final, sin cambiar contenido):

$$
df(S_t,t)
=
\Big[
\frac{\partial f(S_t,t)}{\partial t}
+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_t,t)}{\partial S_t^{2}}(S_t^{2}\sigma^{2})
\Big]dt     
+\frac{\partial f(S_t,t)}{\partial S}dS_t.
$$

---

## 3. (Bivariada, $k=2$) Caso con drift: $dS_t=\mu S_t^3+\big(\tfrac12\big)dt+\sigma^3 dW_t$

Repetimos exactamente la misma estructura del punto 2 (Taylor bivariada de orden 2).  

### 3.1 Taylor de orden 2

Sea $f(S_t,t)$ una función continua con $S_1,t_1\in\mathbb{R}^{+}$ y $k=2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(S_t,t)
&=
f(S_1,t_1)
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t}(S_t-S_1)
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t}(t-t_1) \\
&\quad
+\frac{1}{2!}\Big[
\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}(S_t-S_1)^{2}
+\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t^{2}}(t-t_1)^{2}
+2\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t\partial t}(S_t-S_1)(t-t_1)
\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

Ahora si $t_1 \to t\ \Rightarrow\ t-t_1=\Delta t;\ S_t-S_1=\Delta S_t;\ f(S_t,t)-f(S_1,t_1)=\Delta f(S_t,t)$.

Por tanto,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f(S_t,t)
&=
\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t}\Delta S_t
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t}\Delta t \\
&\quad
+\frac{1}{2}\Big[
\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}(\Delta S_t)^{2}
+\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t^{2}}(\Delta t)^{2}
+\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t\partial t}(\Delta S_t)(\Delta t)
\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

Entonces si $\Delta t \to 0 \Rightarrow \Delta t=dt;\ \Delta S_t=dS_t;\ \Delta f(S_t,t)=df(S_t,t)$.

Por tanto,

$$
\begin{aligned}
df(S_t,t)
&=
\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t}dS_t
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t}dt \\
&\quad
+\frac{1}{2}\Big[
\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}(dS_t)^{2}
+\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t^{2}}(dt)^{2}
+\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t\partial t}(dS_t)(dt)
\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Sustituyendo $dS_t=\mu S_t^{3}+\sigma^{3}dW_t+\frac{1}{2}dt$

como sabemos el proceso de difusión

$$
dS_t=\mu S_t^{3}+\sigma^{3}dW_t+\frac{1}{2}dt
$$

Aplicado en el proceso anterior tenemos que

$$
\begin{aligned}
df(S_t,t)
&=
\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t}dS_t
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t}dt \\
&\quad
+\frac{1}{2}\Big[
\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}
\Big(\mu S_t^{3}+\sigma^{3}dW_t+\frac{1}{2}dt\Big)^{2}
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t\partial t}
\Big(\mu S_t^{3}+\sigma^{3}dW_t+\frac{1}{2}dt\Big)(dt)
\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

donde

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.-\Big(\mu S_t^{3}+\sigma^{3}dW_t+\frac{1}{2}dt\Big)^{2}
&=
(\mu S_t^{3})^{2}+(\sigma^{3}dW_t)^{2}+\Big(\frac{1}{2}dt\Big)^{2}
+2\mu S_t^{3}\sigma^{3}dW_t \\
&\quad
+2\mu S_t^{3}\frac{1}{2}dt
+2\frac{1}{2}\sigma^{3}dW_tdt \\
&=
(\mu S_t^{3})^{2}+\sigma^{6}dt+2\mu S_t^{3}+\mu S_t^{3}dt
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.-\Big(\mu S_t^{3}+\sigma^{3}dW_t+\frac{1}{2}dt\Big)(dt)
&=
\mu S_t^{3}dt+\sigma^{3}dW_tdt+\frac{1}{2}dtdt \\
&=
\mu S_t^{3}dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Sustituyendo, tenemos que

$$
\begin{aligned}
df(S_t,t)
&=
\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t}dS_t
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t}dt \\
&\quad
+\frac{1}{2}\Big[
\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}
\Big((\mu S_t^{3})^{2}+\sigma^{6}dt+2\mu S_t^{3}+\mu S_t^{3}dt\Big)
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t\partial t}\mu S_t^{3}dt
\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
df(S_t,t)
&=
\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t}dS_t
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t}dt
+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}\mu^{2}S_t^{6}
+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}\sigma^{6}dt \\
&\quad
+\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}\mu S_t^{3}
+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}\mu S_t^{3}dt
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t\partial t}\mu S_t^{3}dt
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&=
\Big[
\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial t}
+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}\sigma^{6}
+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}\mu S^{3}
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t\partial t}\mu S^{3}
\Big]dt \\
&\quad
+\frac{\partial f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t}dS_t
+\frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}\mu^{2}S^{6}
+\frac{\partial^{2}f(S_1,t_1)}{\partial S_t^{2}}\mu S_t^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$
